data.title: Lonza Group Continues To Leverage Biopharma's Optimization Push
data.description:
More and more it feels like companies across industries have bought into the idea that it is often best to specialize in whatever it is they do best and outsource as much of what remains as is practical. In the case of pharmaceutical and biotech companies (“biopharma” from here on), companies have increasingly adopted the view that their expertise is in R&D and/or marketing, and manufacturing is a cost center where the risk/reward is skewed toward more things going wrong than adding benefits.

That, in turn, continues to drive strong demand for Lonza Group's (LZAGY) (LZAGF) (LONN.SW) contract development and manufacturing organization (or CDMO) services—effectively, Lonza manufactures drugs for biopharma companies on a contracted outsourcing basis. Moreover, as drug manufacturing becomes ever more complex and geopolitics encourage more local/regional supply resiliency, Lonza’s global scale and comfort with complex processes become even more valuable.

It’s been a while since I’ve updated my thoughts on Lonza, but the company has continued to perform well, particularly since it has chosen to focus on its high-value CDMO operations. The Swiss shares are up more than 200% since my last article, which is quite a bit better than the broader pharmaceutical and biotech sectors, but not quite as good as Thermo Fisher (TMO), WuXi Biologics (WXXWY), or Samsung Biologics (207920.KS). At this point valuation is a concern, but Lonza continues to offer an unusual combination of revenue growth, margin leverage, and FCF upside, as well as a widening moat tied to the complexity and cost of full-scale bioproduction.

Closing The Year On A Strong Note

Lonza, like a fair number of Swiss companies, only reports financial results twice a year. Lonza’s second-half results were once again strong, with small top-line beats complemented by better-than-expected margin leverage.

Revenue rose 15%, beating expectations by about 2%. With the Capsule and Health Ingredients (or CHI) business now treated as a discontinued operation (with a sale expected at some point in 2026), it is the performance of the CDMO operations that really matters now. CDMO revenue was up almost 17% overall, beating by about 1%, with 18%-plus growth in Integrated Biologics and Advanced Synthesis and a 2% decline in Specialized Modalities.

The weaker performance in Specialized has more to do with the ongoing ups and downs of cell and gene therapies at this point rather than any execution issue. Cell and gene therapies are a relatively small segment of biopharma today, and sales for those products that have made it through approval have by and large not really lived up to expectations, as reimbursement has remained challenging in many cases.

EBITDA rose almost 26% for the full company and about 28% for the CDMO business, with margins improving from 29.7% last year to 32.8% this quarter.

Free cash flow for the full year was once again negative, the fifth straight year of that being the case. About 40% of Lonza’s capex is going toward maintenance (which is largely nondiscretionary spending), and the rest goes to the company’s ongoing efforts to grow the business and take advantage of ongoing growth in biopharma outsourcing.

Mixed Guidance, But No Real Fundamental Worries

For a stock with high expectations baked into the valuation, it is often the case that any hiccups in guidance become a big problem for the shares. Not so with Lonza, though, as the Street seemed fine with management’s explanation for its projections for 2026.

Lonza is expecting around 11% to 12% core constant currency growth in its CDMO business in 2026. That’s a roughly 2% cut relative to prior sell-side expectations, but it sounds like it’s all basically tied to foreign exchange, and the Street usually forgives that particular driver. I expect some ongoing caution or concern around the Specialized Modalities business, but management does expect improvement here, and the company did also announce a fifth significant contract for its new Vacaville facility, so utilization there should ramp nicely.

On the margin side, management raised guidance, with an expected CDMO EBITDA margin of over 32% versus analyst expectations of a little over 31%.

Lonza should see free cash flow go positive next year, as capex is expected to decline into the high teens (as a percentage of revenue), basically holding steady while EBITA should grow double digits.

Little Reason To Expect The Flywheel To Slow

I believe Lonza is a leading player in a market that is only getting more attractive over time. Depending upon how you size the market, Lonza has around 8% to 10% share in the CDMO market (closer to 6% share based on management’s estimate of the addressable market), and the company is particularly strong in mammalian cell culture, where it has around one-third of U.S. capacity share and likely something on the order of 50% share of all outsourced business.

Lonza has benefited greatly from increased manufacturing outsourcing across the biopharma industry, and I see no reason to believe that this trend is going to change.

For biotechs, outsourcing manufacturing is practically mandatory. Funds are almost always tight, and it makes no sense for a research-phase company to divert cash toward proprietary manufacturing capacity. Even as these companies commercialize their compounds, it often makes much more sense to reinvest cash into R&D instead of taking production in-house.

It’s a different calculation for larger pharmaceutical companies, but ultimately often the same conclusion. In most cases, manufacturing isn’t a source of value creation—it's something that has to be done, but it’s an area where more can go wrong than go right. What’s more, the markets are increasingly focused on margin maximization and cash returns from Big Pharma, and in most cases, it makes more sense to refocus around R&D and marketing (where these companies can and do add value) and outsource the manufacturing to reduce capex demands.

Ultimately, Lonza benefits from scale and global diversification. With operations across Asia, Europe, and the U.S., Lonza offers regional manufacturing flexibility for drug companies (something that’s getting a lot more attention these days) and more scale efficiencies than all but the largest pharmas could hope to achieve on their own. As such, it ends up being a win-win for both Lonza and the customer.

The Outlook

I don’t really see many risks to Lonza’s growth runway. I suppose it’s possible that U.S. drug pricing reform could reduce R&D investment into new drug development and put some pressure on manufacturing margins, but I don’t find it particularly likely. I do see some risk from more competitors entering the market, but Lonza has been more focused on higher-value, higher-margin manufacturing, and this is an area where newcomers are likely going to find it harder to compete (biopharma will go with trusted providers for particularly complex processes).

I’m expecting low double-digit revenue growth across the next three and five years, and long-term growth likewise just over the double digits. I expect EBITDA margin to get to around 35% over the next five years, and I expect a meaningful ramp in free cash flow margins from around 4% in 2026 to around 8% in 2030 and over 11% in 2035, driving over 20% annualized FCF growth from 2026 onward.

Even with robust revenue growth and reaching double-digit FCF margins, it’s difficult to call Lonza any kind of bargain on discounted cash flow, and that does concern me. On the other hand, looking at the company’s margins, returns, and growth, I can live with multiples of around 6.3x-6.4x on revenue and 20x on EBITDA, both of which support a fair value of around CHF 650/share (or about 20% upside).

The Bottom Line

I can’t call Lonza a conventional bargain, but I wouldn’t expect a well-known company with ongoing double-digit revenue growth, 30%+ EBITDAs (that are still expanding), and significant FCF leverage to trade cheaply. I’d prefer to buy in the high CHF 400s, but who wouldn’t rather get a quality stock at a cheaper price? As is, I think Lonza is a solid enough idea today, though I do worry that the stock could drift unless and until management finds a new growth driver, as I do think the story is well understood by the Street.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: If You Don't Believe In Geo-Capex, Look At Caterpillar's Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Caterpillar's performance has been a true gold mine for its shareholders. Since I rated it as a buy on April 6, 2025, during the "Liberation Day" sell-off, the stock has returned over 125%. In truth, I have been quite surprised by the returns of the stock.

On one side, it was clear that Caterpillar (CAT) could not be viewed only as an excavator manufacturer. First of all, it is an industrial company that has truly developed a service business, which accounts for over a third of its revenues ($24B out of $65B at the end of FY24). This puts Caterpillar in a league of its own. Secondly, its business is also exposed to mining and to AI. In fact, Caterpillar benefits from multiple aspects of the current AI capex cycle. The infrastructure buildout comes immediately to mind. But we also have to consider that AI hardware needs rare earth mining. Finally, we saw how the company's largest business is now its Energy and Transportation division, where its power generation solutions are simply on a roll, with a massive backlog that is building quarter after quarter (this explains the image I used below the title).

We should not end here. There are several additional megatrends that Caterpillar is fully aware of. If you read below, we see that global construction spend is expected to grow 25% over the next decade, while civil infrastructure will rise by 35%. We will see an even faster pace in mining and minerals. Moreover, Caterpillar confirms what I have been saying for a while: oil and gas are not going away anytime soon.

However, my recent rating has been neutral, as I thought that the stock had reached its fair value range, which I considered to be between $450 and $525. Well, the stock is now around $670 and I am among the ones who have missed it. As a matter of fact, I have often made this mistake with Caterpillar. I have often considered it close to its fair value and have thus never initiated a position. It is probably my costliest mistake in my investing career.

Caterpillar has been able to execute perfectly and has ridden the favorable waves of an environment where capex is becoming a strategic priority (we recently talked about geo-capex as the shape of the investing landscape) by leveraging and enhancing its product portfolio. The result is that we see constantly growing operating profit margins and steadily growing cash flows. On top of this, Caterpillar has a shareholder-friendly policy that returns over 100% of its industrial FCF.

Caterpillar just reported its Q4 2025 earnings and, unsurprisingly, the stock is up around 4% as investors welcome the positive news.

Let's take a closer look.

Caterpillar's Q4 2025 Earnings Review

Caterpillar reported quarterly revenues of $19.1B (+18% YoY) and closed the year at $67.6B (+4% YoY). Service revenues for the year were $24B. However, the company's operating profit margin was 13.9%, while a year ago it was 18%. As a result, Caterpillar's EPS declined from $5.78 to $5.12. Enterprise-level operating cash flow was $11.7B for the full year.

Even though Caterpillar's EPS was down YoY, the company beat expectations, reporting an earnings surprise that is quite meaningful: $0.45 above the consensus average of $4.71, representing a surprise close to 10%.

The main driver of the increase in sales was volume, while price realization contributed only $38M, which means that it was basically flat, or even negative when adjusted for inflation.

When we break down Caterpillar's results by segment, we see that Construction Industries reported revenues of $6.9B, up 15% YoY, Resource Industries reached $3.4B, representing a 13% increase YoY and then Power and Energy kept trouncing expectations, reaching $9.4B, a 23% YoY increase.

If we look at the different geographies, we see that North America was up 27% YoY and contributed with $9.7B. Latin America was also up 12% to $1.9B, EAME increased by 19% to 3.8B. Asia Pacific was flat at $2.9B. When we use a segment-regions matrix, we end up with the results that are shown below, which offer us further confirmation of a key fact: construction in North America is strong and is linked to data centers, as power generation confirms.

Now, we saw that Caterpillar's sales were strong, but that its profit margins declined. It is quite easy to guess why: input costs. Caterpillar confirms this when it releases its operating profit bridge. Manufacturing costs represented a $1B headwind, which was barely offset by growing volumes. While Construction Industries and Resource Industries both saw a profit decline (-12% and -24%, respectively), Power and Energy managed to increase its profits by 55%. Clearly, this is what is exciting investors right now, as data centers need Caterpillar's power generation solutions as we need bread.

This wasn't a surprise. Caterpillar has explained throughout last year that tariffs would have represented a headwind. But investors aren't really focused on this. What matters more is its future and, to this extent, Caterpillar delivered better than expected, reporting a record backlog of $51.2B, up 71% YoY. Yes, this is not a typo: 71% in a year.

This is yet another number that confirms that when we are talking about an industrial revolution and a massive capex cycle, it is not only circular accounting. There is real money that is being deployed and Caterpillar's numbers are there to prove it. Furthermore, Caterpillar's inventory of machines declined by $0.5B YoY, which means that demand is real and that the company could flex some pricing power down the road.

What is making investors even more bullish is that Caterpillar now feels confident enough to increase its 2030 targets compared to the guidance it issued last year. Instead of committing to a 4% sales CAGR, it can now expect a growth rate between 5% and 7%, with an adj. operating profit margin forecasted to stay between 15% and 19% at $60B in revenues, with the optionality of possibly reaching the 21%-25% range in case Caterpillar achieves the $100B in revenues milestone.

A few years ago, $100B in sales would have seemed impossible, but given the backlog the company is reporting, I think this could be a target that Caterpillar will achieve by the end of this decade.

In particular, there is another catalyst that investors might not fully appreciate yet. While they are mostly on board with the power generation narrative, many are still not aware that Caterpillar's autonomous haul trucks business is picking up traction and is expected to triple compared to 2024 levels (in 2025, the company grew this business by 20%, so we still have ample upside). In comparison, power generation is "only" expected to double through 2030, using 2024 as the baseline.

Caterpillar's 2026 outlook is convincing. The company expects sales growth around the top end of the new range, with service revenues that will grow YoY. However, we will still see a $2.6B hit from tariffs, which will keep the company's operating margins in the low-teens.

Caterpillar's Valuation

Here comes the tricky part. The risk of FOMO is right around the corner, which is even stronger for me, since I have been covering this stock since it was trading below $200 and have never bought it.

Right now, however, the stock trades at a fwd PE close to 35, and a fwd EV/EBITDA of 25. As much as I like Caterpillar and as much as I like the numbers it is reporting, I see a lot of optimism baked in. This is not without reason, as I have tried to show you. But I think that it is hard to find a margin of safety right now, as the stock is reflecting strong expectations, which pushed its fwd PEG ratio to 5.6. The dividend yield, which once used to be above 2%, is now 0.92%, positioning Caterpillar's multiples and key valuation metrics at par with the best of Big Tech.

I have to congratulate all those who bought Caterpillar's shares because their investment is generating huge returns. But I don't think it would be wise for investors who stayed on the sidelines to jump in now. As a result, I stick to a neutral rating, although I am open to any objection that will come from the comments.

data.title: Netflix Has Further To Fall
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) is an almost $400 billion media streaming conglomerate. The company has underperformed the market by almost 40% since we recommended selling as having much further to fall. Despite that weakness and the potential of an intelligent Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition, we still see the company as having hefty weaknesses ahead.

Netflix Warner Bros. Discovery

Netflix is working to acquire Warner Bros. in a competitive bidding war with Paramount.

The company has announced a $27.75 per share all-cash transaction, a massive $83 billion all-cash transaction. This is huge for Netflix, which would get access to the massive HBO streaming portfolio and, to some extent, make Netflix in the streaming world what cable companies were at one point before as bundlers.

Exactly how this pans out remains to be seen, but it's roughly 10 years of Netflix's FCF, a massive transaction that could take decades to pay out.

Netflix Results

Financially, Netflix has continued to grow revenue, but the timeline to shareholder returns is questionable.

Versus an almost $400 billion market cap, the company's annualized revenue is approaching $50 billion with double-digit YoY growth. However, revenue growth seems to be slowing down substantially going into Q1'26, with less than 1% QoQ growth for the company. Operating margin remains volatile based on the spending in capex.

The company has roughly $9 billion in annual FCF, a 2.2% FCF yield. The company has benefited from growing revenue, but with revenue growth slowing down and the company's dominant position, growing FCF to justify its valuation seems questionable.

Eventually the company needs a high single-digit FCF to justify its valuation versus the S&P 500, and its path to that seems questionable.

Netflix Performance

Netflix continues to have knock-out blockbusters like Wednesday and Kpop Demon Hunters.

These are shows and movies that get a substantial number of viewers; a number of these movies would be blockbusters as movies in theaters. However, it's worth noting that with Netflix already having a massive business, a lot of these are being watched and expected by existing Netflix customers. That's a massive production budget that is required just to keep existing customers.

Netflix is at close to $20 billion in annual content spending, and that's the content that users now expect as the minimum. That is incredibly expensive for the company at $48 billion in annual revenue. With the company already capturing a substantial % of the market, future growth is more questionable to justify FCF growth in our view.

Netflix has had impressive performance across the board; however, there's still some concern. The company's largest segment is UCAN, a market where Netflix is well known and already very established. That means the company's ability to continue long-term growth here is minimal, without major population growth or opportunities.

While Netflix continues to spend massively on content, it hasn't seen massive customer growth as a result, and this concentrated revenue is concerning in the company's ability to increase FCF.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is reducing competition. Netflix acquiring Warner Bros. Discovery would substantially reduce competition and give the company a near-untouchable portfolio. That would enable it to increase bundle prices higher and higher, which, with fixed content costs, could enable it to substantially grow cash flow.

Conclusion

Netflix has underperformed the market substantially since we recommended against investing. That's not surprising; the company's brand and scale took on massive popularity, popularity that wasn't reflected in the company's core financial results. The company has struggled to get the FCF to justify its valuation, and the 40% decline reflects that.

Now the company is going through a potentially massive acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery. While we think that's an intelligent acquisition, it's one that's expensive, one that will take a number of years for the company to be able to pay off. At the same time, with weak FCF, we continue to recommend against investing in Netflix.

data.title: Tesla: Overpriced, Yet No Clear Catalyst To Break It
data.description:
Writing about Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is always a funny experience. In a company with such polarized opinions, taking a moderate tone is definitely a good way to receive criticism from both bulls and bears.

I believe that in all my years of analyzing companies, I’ve never seen an example where the recent fundamental trajectory is so de-correlated from price action. In other words, a company where investors are willing to bet increasingly larger amounts of money on future promises while the present reality keeps getting worse.

And why is that? Tesla bulls are not investing in the results the company is showing today. They are paying to be a partner of CEO Elon Musk in any of his future endeavors, believing he’s capable of landing any project, even with the timeframe always being questionable. Are they wrong? Only the future will tell...

There’s also a history of Tesla’s price action destroying short sellers, which helps keep the stock unanchored, even when a large number of investors believe the valuation makes no sense.

In my previous article on TSLA, I tried to dissect some of these opportunities, like robotaxis and Optimus, and value them as separate businesses. Of course, there’s a lot of speculation here, since none of these products has a defined business model yet, but I’d invite you to read that article too.

The latest Tesla earnings call was another example of the same debacle between the present and the future, which I’ll be commenting on.

Tesla, Inc. Q4 2025 Earnings Call

Auto sales continue to suffer. Revenue for the quarter reached $24.9 billion, a 3.1% year-over-year decline. The contraction was mostly in the automotive segment, which fell 11% YoY to $17.69 billion. Total deliveries declined 8.6% YoY, after 2024 was already below 2023.

It’s also worth remembering that on the Q3 2024 earnings call, Elon gave a "best guess" of 20% to 30% growth in vehicle sales in 2025. Well, not even close... but who cares, right?

That said, I have to admit automotive margin surprised me positively. It came in at 17.9%, well above estimates in the 14.3% to 15% range. Growing gross margins while revenues are falling is really difficult, and management attributed it to mix, selling more cars in APAC and EMEA, and cost-control initiatives. This surprise helped overall EPS beat the consensus of $0.45 by $0.05, and gross margin reached 20.1%, something Tesla hasn’t achieved in the last two years.

The energy storage business is the only one really showing continuous growth. It came in at $3.8 billion, up 25% YoY and 12.3% QoQ. With expanding margins, this segment is absorbing at least some of the EPS pressure from automotive.

Another impactful update was the revision to 2026 CapEx guidance. Management now says total CapEx in 2026 will be above $20B, which is a massive step up from the roughly $9B they were pointing to in Q3 2025 (more than a 100% jump in the implied run rate) and also well above the $10.9B consensus.

The spend is basically going toward scaling "Physical AI" including six new production lines (the refinery, LFP factories, Cybercab, Semi, a new Megafactory, and the Optimus factory) and a major expansion of the AI training cluster. That’s a bullish signal of management’s confidence in the long-term thesis.

My Take

Even if I’m impressed with the gross margins Tesla achieved, I can’t praise a result that showed more than a 16% QoQ decline in automotive revenue. In Europe, the situation is especially hard, with deliveries down 27.8% YoY.

On top of that, the phase-out of U.S. credits should keep pressuring the automotive business, both on the demand side and on margins.

I still think the best way to value TSLA is to estimate what the current businesses are worth and then back into the price the market is paying for optionalities. With automotive shrinking, those optionalities get more expensive at the same stock price, and even more so if the stock keeps going up.

I’ll also admit I’ve been optimistic myself about the car business alone (not considering robotaxis, Optimus, etc.), building my base case around 10 million vehicles. That scenario seems almost impossible now. Depending on how you value the auto business, and here I’ll invite you to my latest article again, the car business alone can be worth "only" $40 per share, or about 10% of today’s price. With my base case for energy at around $60, almost 75% of the stock price now depends on future initiatives.

On the Robotaxi front, Elon seems to be very confident and stated that the first rides with no safety monitors in the car occurred in Austin earlier this month, and that's basically what believers want to hear. They also plan to expand to seven more U.S. cities in the first half of 2026.

Another positive development is that active FSD subscriptions have raised from 0.8M in 2024 to 1.1M last year, a substantial increase but a small portion of the ~7.5mi vehicles sold in the last five years.

And he's really doubling down on this path, bringing the flagship Model S and Model X programs to an end next quarter to transition resources toward autonomy.

It’s hard to estimate how much robotaxi revenue could impact earnings because the business model is not clear yet. Will Tesla operate the fleet? Will it sell or lease cars to third-party operators? In my previous work, I got to something like 8M vehicles on the road to justify a ~$500-ish value. Producing that amount of cars doesn’t happen overnight, so whether it gets there or not, I don’t know. But I’d bet it would take time before earnings multiples look reasonable at today’s price. SA's P/E Non-GAAP (FY3) estimate of 90.1 corroborates that idea.

All things considered, I tend to think there’s too much future hope being pulled into TSLA’s present value. Valuing robotaxis and humanoids business is more of a venture-capital exercise than a traditional public-equity one. You can try to estimate total addressable market, share, and unit economics, but the uncertainty is massive.

As for me, I’d argue these future possibilities are already too expensive to buy, especially with the automotive business becoming less and less valuable. On the other hand, I don’t see a reason to put a Sell rating on it because I don’t see what would actually make the stock fall.

Bulls aren’t sensitive at all to bad earnings, and bears aren’t willing to short it either (Seeking Alpha’s analyst Sell rating is 2.06 out of 5, but short interest is only about 2%, per SA). It would take a real negative development on the robotaxi front to send shares down, like a major accident or something. And even so, there's also the Optimus narrative.

Besides, falling interest rates should help car sales to some extent in 2026 and beyond.

Conclusion

To wrap it up, TSLA is still very expensive relative to what the business is delivering today, with the stock priced as if robotaxis and “physical AI” are already de-risked. But it also feels like it has no clear levers to re-rate down.

The stock is anchored to optionality, not near-term fundamentals, which makes a Sell hard to justify. I stay at Hold, with falling rates as a modest tailwind, but valuation is still dominated by future bets.

data.title: SLVO: An Income Oriented Strategy For The Silver Trade
data.description:
The UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO) is an exchange-traded note designed to provide investors with monthly income derived from option premiums received through a covered call strategy on the iShares Silver Trust (SLV). With the price per ounce of silver reaching new highs, investors may need to weigh how the performance of SLVO is influenced by the price of silver and whether this ETN is appropriate for one’s portfolio strategy.

About UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN

SLVO was issued by Credit Suisse on April 16, 2013 and is set to mature on April 21, 2033. The strategy was absorbed by UBS on May 31, 2024 when the two banks merged. On January 28, 2025, Nasdaq became the sole Index Sponsor of the Nasdaq Silver FLOWS 106 Index of which SLVO’s performance was designed to track. SLVO has an annual investor fee rate of 65bps.

Unlike exchange-traded funds, ETNs are debt issuances that represent the performance of the indicated strategy. In addition to this, ETNs mature, meaning that UBS will be obligated to return the notional value of the ETNs to the investors at the end of the term. The value received at maturity is based on the arithmetic average of the preceding 5 trading days upon the maturity date.

Rather than paying out a dividend distribution, the ETN pays out a cash coupon. The cash coupon is paid out monthly and may vary month-to-month based on the strategy’s call premiums received.

SLVO was designed to track the NASDAQ Silver FLOWS [Formula-Linked OverWrite Strategy] 106 Index. The Index was designed to measure the performance of a covered call strategy on SLV, which will perform based on the price returns of SLV shares along with option premiums received net of costs. Essentially, the Index will track a long position in SLV shares along with a short position in call options [short-long]. Call options are sold with a strike price of roughly 106% of the price of SLV shares, or 6% above the trading price when the option is sold. This means that if SLV appreciates beyond 6%, SLV may be called away, limiting the full upside potential of the long-SLV shares position.

What SLVO Is and How It Works

SLVO is essentially a buy-write strategy in which the strategy tracks the performance of the underlying shares while overlaying a covered call options strategy to generate income. As a result of the structure of the strategy, there will be some puts and takes when investing in SLVO.

Option premiums generate monthly income. Long SLV shares provide upside potential. Covered calls may limit upside potential if SLV shares appreciate beyond 6% before the options’ expiration date.

SLVO can be best applied in a flat-to-slow-growth silver market environment where investors may fully benefit from the value appreciation of the SLV shares while gaining income through the covered call strategy. As a result of the covered call strategy, SLVO may fall short in performance during periods of high volatility, particularly when silver appreciates beyond the 6% spread. Because of the bull run that began in September 2025, SLVO has significantly underperformed SLV. This can be observed when comparing 1-year total returns.

Comparing a 1-year period prior to September 2025, SLVO and SLV moved in near tandem, suggesting that the strategy may work best during more normalized trading patterns. Given the structure of the trading strategy, SLVO may outperform SLV during periods of flat, declining, and slow growth for silver prices.

Investor Suitability

SLVO may be most appropriate for buy-and-hold investors seeking monthly income. SLVO pays out monthly coupons; the total coupon rate for the last 12 months was $24.3777/note, which amounts to roughly 24% yield at the current SLVO.

SLVO may not be appropriate as an active investment strategy given the limited upside potential relative to SLV. SLVO has relatively thin liquidity with $344mm in net assets and an average of $4.9mm in value changing hands on a daily basis. This compares to SLV, which has roughly $57b in net assets and an average trading volume of $5.05b in share value.

Risks Related to SLVO

SLVO was designed to provide long exposure to SLV while overlaying a covered call options strategy to generate monthly income, presenting certain risks that investors should consider prior to making a final investment decision. SLVO limits upside potential as a result of the covered call strategy, particularly during periods of heightened volatility to the upside. We can see this in full force over this last year (at the time of writing), where SLV has enjoyed a sizable run-up while SLVO did not participate in the upswing, even when looking at total return (which incorporates reinvested income).

Given the ETN structure, SLVO will mature on April 21, 2033 with no guaranteed minimum payment at maturity. An investment in SLVO is not a direct investment in physical silver, but merely an exchange-traded product designed to track the performance of SLV and a covered call strategy on SLV shares. Given the variability feature of coupon payments, investors are not guaranteed to receive payments. Unlike bonds, investors will not receive the principal amount upon maturity; the ETNs may mature at a price below the investor’s initial investment.

Additionally, it's important to note that the income generated as a result of the short calls creates a volatility-muting effect as reflected in the total return. However, this is not a structural buffer against SLV's downside, as the strategy doesn't involve any long puts that would create a floor. The downside cushioning effect is limited to the income received.

Final Thoughts

SLVO is an ETN designed to provide investors exposure to silver through long exposure to SLV while selling covered call options on SLV to generate monthly income. SLVO may experience limited upside potential during periods of high price appreciation in SLV given the covered call strategy. SLVO may be best utilized during normal silver market operations as exhibited in the charts above.

This article answers three main questions about SLVO:

  1. How does SLVO relate to SLV and what is its purpose?
  2. What risk accompany SLVO?
  3. In which trading environments is SLVO most likely to perform well?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
